ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oyster food product class; no specific class for baked oysters with garlic parsley butter topping. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Oysters — baked preparation, closest FoodEx2 anchor for cooked oyster dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers oysters as a food category; no specific facet code for garlic-parsley-butter baked preparation. Approximate match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for baked oysters with garlic parsley butter; SNOMED CT clinical food coding does not extend to this preparation level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One full order of 12 baked oysters weighs approximately 250 g, representing a shared starter or individual main course portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Oysters, eastern, cooked, breaded and fried (NDB 15169) and Butter, salted (NDB 01001), adapted composite estimate for baked oysters with garlic parsley butter
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for baked oysters with garlic parsley butter and breadcrumbs. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked eastern oysters (NDB 15169) and salted butter (NDB 01001), weighted to reflect the dish composition (approximately 65% oyster, 25% butter/garlic/parsley, 10% breadcrumbs). The input record's per_100g values (energy 211 kcal, protein 11.1 g, fat 15.6 g, carbohydrate 6.7 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained as the best available estimate. Confidence is medium due to the composite nature of the estimate.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate using mixed seafood dish with breadcrumb coating (GI ~40)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baked oysters with garlic parsley butter. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on analogous mixed seafood dishes with breadcrumb coating from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The dominant carbohydrate source is breadcrumbs (refined wheat); however, the low carbohydrate content (6.67 g/100g) and high protein and fat content substantially blunt the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (40 × 6.67 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.7 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.33 g/100g from butter exceeds the AHA threshold of 5 g/100g, contributing to arterial stiffness; sodium at 305.56 mg/100g is borderline and the low potassium and fibre content provide insufficient offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 6 pieces (approximately 125 g), request reduced butter, and confirm low-sodium breadcrumbs are used, as the combined saturated fat and sodium burden may elevate cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content is low at 6.67 g/100g with only 0.56 g/100g sugar; high protein (11.11 g/100g) from oysters slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose rise; estimated GI is low (~40).
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes at a standard 12-piece serving (250 g, approximately 16.7 g total carbohydrate, GL ~6.7); however, requesting wholegrain breadcrumbs and reduced butter will further improve the glycaemic and insulin-resistance profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 8.33 g/100g and trans fat at 0.28 g/100g from butter significantly elevate LDL cholesterol risk; cholesterol content is 55.56 mg/100g; the full 250 g serving delivers approximately 20.8 g saturated fat, well above the AHA daily limit of 13 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the high saturated fat and trans fat content from butter; if consumed, limit to 3–4 pieces and request olive oil substitution for butter to reduce atherogenic lipid load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 8.33 g/100g from butter exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold and promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; oysters provide zinc and some omega-3 but are insufficient to offset the butter-driven inflammatory load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request substitution of butter with extra-virgin olive oil to reduce saturated fat and add anti-inflammatory oleocanthal; garlic and parsley should be retained for their polyphenol content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked Oysters with Garlic Parsley Butter (12 Pieces) is a restaurant dish consisting of twelve fresh oysters baked in their half-shells and topped with a compound butter made from garlic, flat-leaf parsley, and salted butter, finished with a breadcrumb crust that provides a golden, crispy texture. The dish is commonly served as a starter or shared appetiser in Japanese-influenced seafood restaurants in Singapore. The flavour profile is rich, savoury, and umami-forward, with the briny sweetness of the oyster balanced by the aromatic garlic-herb butter and the textural contrast of toasted breadcrumbs. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy at approximately 211 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of 11.1 g per 100 g from the oysters, which are a natural source of zinc, iron (3.33 mg/100g), and vitamin B12. The fat content is high at 15.56 g per 100 g, predominantly saturated fat (8.33 g/100g) from the butter, which raises cardiovascular and hypertension concerns at full-serving quantities. Carbohydrate content is low at 6.67 g per 100 g, making the glycaemic load per 250 g serving approximately 6.7 (low). Sodium is borderline at 305.56 mg per 100 g. The dish is not suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis at full portions due to the saturated fat load, but is generally acceptable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes given the low carbohydrate and low glycaemic index profile. Allergens include shellfish, dairy, and gluten from breadcrumbs.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central composite estimates for cooked eastern oysters and salted butter, weighted by estimated dish composition. The input record values are consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is medium due to the composite and estimated nature of the nutritional data.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific baked oyster with garlic parsley butter preparation are not available as a composite entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2024. Individual USDA entries for oysters and butter provide partial data but cannot be reliably combined at this level of detail without introducing unacceptable estimation error. 2. The input record listed 'Japanese' as primary cuisine with ethnic tags including 'Alcoholic Drink' and 'Japanese Rice Bowl', which are clearly erroneous for this dish. These have been corrected: the dish is a Western-style baked oyster preparation served in a Japanese restaurant context in Singapore; cuisine is classified as Japanese-Western Fusion / Restaurant Dish. 3. Two portions were listed in the input with both marked is_default=true; the 12-piece (250 g) portion is used as the canonical serving size. 4. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to the HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database and is not an approved primary nutrient source; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central as the approved source. 5. GI is estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on analogous mixed seafood dishes with breadcrumb coating per the University of Sydney GI Database framework and Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in approved sources. 6. Multilingual local names: this dish is served in a Singapore Japanese-restaurant context. Per G6 rules, the dish is treated as a Singapore restaurant dish requiring English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi coverage, plus Japanese for the Japanese cuisine context. Malay: 'Tiram Bakar dengan Mentega Bawang Putih dan Pasli (12 Ketul)'; Tamil: 'வெள்ளுள்ளி வெண்ணெய் சுட்ட சிப்பி (12 துண்டுகள்)'; Hindi: 'लहसुन अजमोद मक्खन के साथ बेक्ड ऑयस्टर (12 टुकड़े)'. These are descriptive translations provided for Singapore market retrieval coverage. 7. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array. Note: the Tamil alias in aliases_by_language uses the native-script form 'வெள்ளுள்ளி வெண்ணெய் சுட்ட சிப்பி (12 துண்டுகள்)' which appears in the flat aliases array; a romanized Tamil variant 'லஸுன் அஜ்மோத் மக்கன் கே சாத் பேக்ட் ஆய்ஸ்டர்' is also included in the flat aliases array as an additional retrieval aid.
